coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of value-added nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots from Crescentia cujete fruit waste for selective sensing of Fe3+ ions in aqueous medium
coredata.dc:creator: Tony Elizabeth, A.
James, E.
Infant Jesan, L.
Denis Arockiaraj, S.
Edwin Vasu, Antonisamy
coredata.dc:description: Green synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) by one-pot hydrothermal synthesis was reported using Crescentia cujete fruit waste for the first time in this study. The spherical carbon dots obtained are loaded with carboxyl, hydroxyl and amino functional groups on their surface which make them highly dispersible in water and emit green fluorescent light under UV irradiation. The photoluminescence (PL) behaviour of N-CQDs is greatly reduced by Fe3+ ions because of strong excited state electron transfer. This fluorescent “turn-off” behaviour towards Fe3+ is highly selective and sensitive with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.257 μM. The highly stable N-CQDs were also successfully used as promising fluorescent probes for the sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions in three real waters, namely, river water, laboratory water and tap water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Fluorescent “turn-off” sensor
Fe3+ ions
Crescentia cujete fruit
Hydrothermal synthesis

Fluorescent “turn-off” sensing and detection of ferric ions
Effect of pH and time on PL intensity of N-CQDs
Application of N-CQDs for the fluorescence sensing of Fe3+ in real wastewaters

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The HR-TEM image of N-CQDs with particle size distribution (inset). (b) HR-TEM image showing the lattice spacing in spherical N-CQDs with lattice fringes (inset).
XPS spectra of N-CQDs. (a) Full XPS spectrum. (b-d). Deconvoluted C1s, N1s and O1s spectra.
(a). UV–visible spectrum of N-CQDs. (b). Excitation-emission spectra (Inset: photographs of N-CQDs under day light and UV light).
(a). PL spectra of N-CQDs in the presence different metal cations. (b). The selectivity of N-CQDs to different metal cations.
(a). PL spectra of N-CQDs with different Fe3+ concentrations. (b). Fluorescence response of N-CQDs as a function of Fe3+ concentration.
(a). Effect of pH on PL intensity of N-CQDs. (b). Stability of N-CQDs.
Comparison of LOD values of N-CQDs for Fe3+ ions with similar carbon dots reported recently.
Application of N-CQDs for the detection of Fe3+ ions in real waters.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of value-added nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots from Crescentia cujete fruit waste for selective sensing of Fe3+ ions in aqueous medium

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Holy Cross College (Autonomous), Affiliated to Bharathidasan University, Tiruchirappalli 620 002, Tamil Nadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Holy Cross College (Autonomous), Affiliated to Bharathidasan University, Tiruchirappalli 620 002, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, St. Joseph’s College (Autonomous), Affiliated to Bharathidasan University, Tiruchirappalli 620 002, Tamil Nadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, St. Joseph’s College (Autonomous), Affiliated to Bharathidasan University, Tiruchirappalli 620 002, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Green synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) by one-pot hydrothermal synthesis was reported using Crescentia cujete fruit waste for the first time in this study. The spherical carbon dots obtained are loaded with carboxyl, hydroxyl and amino functional groups on their surface which make them highly dispersible in water and emit green fluorescent light under UV irradiation. The photoluminescence (PL) behaviour of N-CQDs is greatly reduced by Fe3+ ions because of strong excited state electron transfer. This fluorescent “turn-off” behaviour towards Fe3+ is highly selective and sensitive with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.257 μM. The highly stable N-CQDs were also successfully used as promising fluorescent probes for the sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions in three real waters, namely, river water, laboratory water and tap water.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: All living organisms require Fe3+ ion as it plays vital role in many metabolic processes like transport of molecular oxygen, nucleic acids synthesis and electron transports. In addition, living cells require the presence of these ions during infection to sustain their function, metabolism and proliferation [1]. But excessive accumulation of Fe3+ higher than the physiological cut off values affects almost all types of cellular structures and activities in organs including heart, pancreas, kidney, liver and others [2]. Iron overload also affects bone marrow function [3] and causes cancer [4]. Though Fe3+ accumulation in humans is mainly linked to contaminated food products [5], its origin traces back to the leaching of iron from weathering of iron silicates and other minerals into the aquatic environment in the form of hydrated iron oxide [6]. As ferric ions are also toxic to fish and other aquatic organisms [7], it is necessary to develop appropriate, rapid monitoring techniques for their presence even at very low concentration levels like those in natural waters. Among the existing techniques, spectrophotometry, flow-injection analysis, graphite furnace atomic absorption, high-resolution electrospray ionization mass spectrometry and others, fluorescent method [8,9,10] is considered to be the most suitable because of its rapidity, high sensitivity and selectivity and free from tedious pre-treatment methods. Though there are various “turn-on” fluorescent probes available for Fe (III) [11], numerous works have recently appeared which make use of carbon quantum dots as “turn-off” sensors for many metal ions including Fe3+ [12,13].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are quasi-spherical particles with sizes less than 10 nm and have been the subject of many recent scientific reports as they possess many peculiar properties like hydrophilicity, biocompatibility, low toxicity, and photoluminescence. They have potential optical and electrical applications in many important fields, particularly, energy and bioengineering [14,15,16]. The PL property of carbon dots is mainly due to the presence of surface groups like carboxyl, hydroxyl and others that are attached to a nanocarbon core and can be altered/modified by such a procedure as doping with a heteroatom like nitrogen. Carbon dots of these types are highly sensitive to ions like ferric, which seriously reduce their PL intensity by means of establishing strong interactions with the functional groups in them. Though carbon dots can be synthesized from many small organic molecules like glucose, urea, organic acids and others [17], a huge opportunity is available for their preparation from many cheap, green precursors like biomasses or extracts of biomasses that are rich in carbohydrate and natural organic products content [18,19].

Crescentia cujete is widely distributed and cultivated in many parts of the world ranging from South America, tropical Africa and South-east Asia. Extracts of stem, bark, leaves and fruits of the plant are used in traditional medicine. The fruit is very large and the pulp is not normally consumed by birds, animals or humans. Presence of plenty of organic compounds like, n-alkyl glycosides, phenylethanoids, flavone and flavanone glycosides, phenyl acid derivatives and iridoids glycosides in the pulp is recently reported [20]. In this context, it is proposed in the present investigation, to utilize the fruit juice of Crescentia cujete for the preparation of nitrogen-dopped carbon dots and to explore the possibility of using them as a fluorescent “turn-off” sensor for Fe3+ ions in pure water and to extend the applicability to real water samples.

Nitrates of Fe3+ and Ag+, chlorides of Hg2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Ca2+ and Na+, acetates of Pb2+, Zn2+ and sulphates of Fe2+, Ni2+ and Mg2+ were purchased from Merk India limited and double distilled water was used for all purposes. All other reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

Fruits of Crescentia cujete were collected locally and the pulp is crushed in a juicer after removing the seeds. 5 mL of this juice was mixed with 25 mL of water and heated in a Teflon-lined autoclave at a temperature of 200 °C for 10 h. After the reaction period, the autoclave was allowed to cool by itself to room temperature and the brown coloured liquid was filtered using a 0.22 μm syringe filter and finally stored in a refrigerator at 5 °C without any further treatment. The as-prepared nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) solution was diluted 100 times before photophysical and sensing studies.

FT-IR spectrum was recorded in a Perkin Elmer Spectrum Two model IR spectrometer. Particle size and morphology of N-CQDs were studied by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, JEOL, JEM-2100) operated at 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectra were recorded using a K-Alpha instrument, XPS KAlpha Surface Analysis, Thermo Fischer Scientific, UK. UV–visible spectral recordings and optical absorptions are done with a Perkin Elmer, Lambda 365 model UV–Visible Spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectra were recorded using a Perkin Elmer Model LS45 spectrofluorometer. Elemental mapping of N-CQDs was performed using SEM (VEGA 3 TESCAN) with EDAX (Bruker).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of Fe3+ ions in ideal and real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the effect of different metal ions (Fe3+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ag+ and Na+) on the fluorescence behaviour of N-CQDs, 1 mL of the latter was mixed with 1 mL of 500 μM metal ion solutions along with a blank and the fluorescent intensities were measured after equilibrating for 15 min. The same procedure is repeated with N-CQDs-Fe3+ mixtures such that the final concentrations of Fe3+ range from 0 to 250 μM. In order to test the applicability in real waters, Cauvery River water collected locally, de-ionized water from a chemistry laboratory and tap water were spiked with ferric ions of 25 μM concentration, equilibrated with N-CQDs, and their effects were studied.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface and morphological characterization of N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Structural morphology and size distributions were found from HR-TEM analysis of N-CQDs. The images show clear evidences for their spherical shape with the average particle size of 4.36 nm (inset in Fig. 2 a). The high-resolution image seen in Fig. 2b reveals a d-spacing of 0.222 nm which is characteristic of the (100) plane of the graphitic layer and is also supported by the fine lattice fringe pattern (inset of Fig. 2b) [24,25].

Presence of three major elements, namely, carbon, nitrogen and oxygen are confirmed by the peaks at 285 eV (C1s), 400 eV (N1s) and 533 eV (O1s) in the X-ray photoelectron spectrum (Fig. 3 a). When deconvoluted, the C1s signal yields four different peaks that are centred at 283.9, 285.4, 286.6 and 289.8 eV which are due to, respectively, C–C/C=C, C–N/C–O, C=O and COOH carbon environments (Fig. 3b). The N1s peak yields a major peak at 399.6 eV which accounts for nitrogen atoms that are connected to carbons and a minor peak at 401.1 eV due to the N–H groupings (Fig. 3c). It is interesting to note that apart from peaks at 531. 2 eV (O-C) and 533.0 eV (O=C) in O1s spectrum, a third peak is found at 534.3 eV which could be due to hydroxy oxygen atoms (Fig. 3d) [26]. The integrated peak areas found for C1s, N1s and O1s from the XPS spectrum were found to be, 59.0%, 3.6% and 37.4%, respectively, thus confirming the presence of doped nitrogen on the carbon dots as revealed by the IR spectrum.

Elemental analysis of N-CQDs have been done by recording the distribution of three major elements, C, N and O. The results (Fig. S1) show the presence of these elements and their relative distribution which are in accordance with FT-IR and XPS results.

Optical properties of N-CQDs were evaluated by recording their absorption in the UV–visible range and studying the emissions in terms of their PL spectra. The UV–visible absorption spectrum (Fig. 4 a) shows three absorptions. Bands at 220 nm and 280 nm are due to π-π* excitations of the aromatic π bonds in the carbon core, while the very small peak around 330 nm is due to the n-π* excitations in carboxyl functional groups that are accumulated at the surface [27,28]. The excitation-emission spectra recorded are shown in Fig. 4b along with the photographs taken for N-CQDs under daylight and UV light irradiation (inset in Fig. 4b). The N-CQDs are yellowish-brown under day light and turned bright green under UV irradiation. The emission maximum was found to be at 422 nm when the exicitation wavelength was at 335 nm. Therefore, the excitatiom maximum was kept at this wavelengh in all further emission studies. Since emission has not occurred when the 280 nm wavelength was used to excite which was the major absorption peak in the UV spectrum (due to π-π* transitions within the carbonic core), it is inferred that the fluorescent emission of N-CQDs is primarily due to the absorption around 335 nm that is due to the n-π* excitations [29]. This green emission is also found to be excitation dependant (Fig. S2) and red shifted from 422 nm to 500 nm with concomittant decrease in intensity when the excitation wavelength was changed gradually from 335 to 465 nm, with increments of 10 nm. This type of behaviour is common among many carbon dots and is believed to be due to different particle sizes of carbon cores and differences in the nature of states on the surface [21].

Fluorescent quantum yields of N-CQDs were determined by the relative method by taking the quantum yield of quinine sulphate (QS) in 0.1 M H2SO4 as 0.54 and making use of the following equation, where φ, η, A and I are the quantum yields, refractive indices, absorbance values and integrated fluorescent intensities, respectively [26].
φ
N - C Q D s
=
φ
QS
×
η
N - C Q D s
2
η
QS
2
×
A
QS
A
NCQD - s
×
I
N - C Q D s
I
QS

Refractive indices for both QS and N-CQD solutions were assumed to be 1.33 and their integrated fluorescent intensities were measured for solutions whose optical density values were less than 0.1, at the excitation wavelength (435 nm), in order to minimize reabsorption effects. The PL spectra recorded for N-CQD and QS whose absorbance values were, respectively, 0.096 and 0.010, are provided in Fig. S3 and the QY was found to be 1.57%. Though the quantum yield of N-CQDs prepared from Crescentia cujete fruits is low compared to dots that are prepared from other fruit juices by hydrothermal method recently, namely, loquat fruit (10.36%) [30], lemon juice (14.86–24.89%) [31], banana juice (32%) [32] and Annona squamosa fruit extract (14.16%) [33], the present study has the advantage of utilizing a merely waste fruit biomass which is of no commercial value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent “turn-off” sensing and detection of ferric ions
ce:section.ce:para: In order to utilize the newly prepared N-CQDs for the fluorescent detection of ferric and other metal ions, solutions of Fe3+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ag+ and Na+ at 250 μM concentrations are equilibrated with carbon dots and their PL intensity is monitored. The results (Fig. 5 a and b) clearly show the unique ability of Fe3+ ions in completely removing the luminescence of carbon dots at these concentrations. Since other metal ions do not significantly affect the PL intensity of N-CQDs while Fe3+ ions are having dramatic effects, it is inferred that there are specific interactions involved between them and as reported in a recent study [34] it could be due to rapid electron transfer from excited states of the dots into vacant d orbitals of Fe3+ ions. It is also worthy to mention here that carboxyl and amino groups on aromatic carbon core of N-CQDs are enriched with excited state electrons after absorption of radiation thereby acting as fluorescent centres. As these interact strongly with the added Fe3+ ions, electron densities are transferred to the vacant orbitals of iron, and de-excitation of fluorophores by light emissions are eventually reduced to an extent that depends on the added amounts of Fe3+ ions.

In order to find the mechanism of sensing of Fe3+ ions by N-CQDs the UV–visible spectra have been recorded for N-CQDs with and without all the metal ions (Fig. S4). It appears from Fig. S4a and Fig. S4b that the mechanism of quenching is likely to be due to inner filter effect [35] because of the high UV absorbance of Fe3+ ions than N-CQDs and other metal ions. But, since dilution helps to reduce the occurrence of these effects, UV–visible spectra were also recorded for five-times diluted Fe3+ ions and Fe3+-N-CQDs mixtures. The results (Fig. S4c) demonstrate the elimination of inner filter effects and show marked changes in the characteristic peak of N-CQDs at about 280 nm and the development of a new peak around 380 nm, both with increased absorbance values. This is a clear indication that there are specific complexing interactions involved between N-CQDs and ferric ions indicating a static quenching mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH and time on PL intensity of N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Before being tested for any practical sensing application, like the detection of Fe3+ ions in real water systems, effect of pH on the PL intensity is to be studied along with the stability with respect to time. The results of our experiments show that though the pH affects the intensity slightly (Fig. 7 a), it is not eliminating the fluorescence property completely and doors are open for its usability for systems with wide pH range. Emission was maximum at a pH of 5 and decreases on either increasing or decreasing the pH beyond this value. Similar observation was recently reported for fluorescent carbon quantum dots synthesized from endosperm of Borassus flabellifer [39]. The PL intensity of N-CQDs is almost unaffected even after 30 days (Fig. 7b), confirming their high solubility in water and ability to sustain and retain their nano level structures in water [44], which are pre-requisites for its candidature to be used as a sensor for the presence of any species in natural waters.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of N-CQDs for the fluorescence sensing of Fe3+ in real wastewaters
ce:section.ce:para: Three real water samples were used to assess the applicability of the synthesised N-CQDs for detection of ferric ions after spiking the same with a chosen amount. The results provided in Table 2 suggest that the newly developed carbon dots can be successfully used for the sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions at micromolar levels. It is to be noted that lower recovery rates are found for river and laboratory water samples whose pH values are greater than 8, while recovery rates of tap water are the best among the three whose pH value of 6.9 is the closest to pH 5 at which N-CQDs have maximum PL intensity.

Herein, we report a simple, one-pot, bottom-up synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots from a green carbon resource, Crescentia cujete fruit juice. The prepared carbon dots have been systematically analyzed for their structural, morphological, chemical and optical characteristics. The as-prepared N-CQDs exhibit green fluorescence because of the presence of functional groups like –COOH, –NH2 that are attached on the surface of spherical carbon core particles. Presence of doped nitrogen and surface groups were confirmed by IR and X-ray photoelectron spectra. HR-TEM images confirmed the well-defined graphitic layers within the carbon core whose average particle size was 4.36 nm. The green emission of N-CQDs was greatly quenched in the presence of Fe3+ ions as they were able to transfer electrons from the excited fluorophores at the surface into the vacant d orbitals of iron. The PL response of N-CQDs is linear with up to 25 μM concentration of Fe3+ and showed a very good LOD value of 0.257. Promising results were obtained when the applicability of newly developed N-CQDs as highly sensitive fluorescent “turn-off” probe for the detection of Fe3+ ions was tested with river, laboratory and tap waters.

A. Tony Elizabeth: Conceptualization, Methodology. E. James: Investigation. L. Infant Jesan: Investigation. S. Denis Arockiaraj: Methodology, Formal analysis. Antonisamy Edwin Vasu: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2023.110427.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
